title: Treacle Sponge Pudding
subtitle: Steamed golden-syrup sponge with toffee sauce
excerpt: A classic British steamed pudding: a butter sponge poured over a pool of golden syrup and steamed until soft and yielding. Served with proper custard or extra syrup poured over at the table.
sourceNotes: • Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), §1302, 1310, steamed puddings chapter, suet sponges and golden-syrup variants. Project Gutenberg #10136. Modern butter-sponge adaptation derived from the proportions in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), pudding chapter, Project Gutenberg.

content.text: Treacle sponge is one of the great British steamed puddings. A soft butter sponge sits in a basin over a thick pool of golden syrup, then steams for ninety minutes until the sponge swells and the syrup turns to something close to toffee at the base. Turn it out onto a warmed plate and that golden syrup runs down over the dome like a glaze.

The recipe uses the standard creaming-method sponge: butter, sugar, eggs, flour. It is lighter and more reliable in a home kitchen than the older suet versions. Steaming is the key. The enclosed, moist heat gives the sponge its particular softness that oven baking cannot match.

A 1.2-litre pudding basin is the right size for six people. Smaller basins work but require the cooking time adjusted downward to about 60 minutes. Serve with crème anglaise, double cream poured cold over the hot pudding, or extra golden syrup warmed in a small pan.
What you need

You need a 1.2-litre pudding basin with a lid, or cover tightly with a double layer of foil pleated across the top to allow for expansion. A large saucepan deep enough that the basin sits on a trivet or folded cloth with the lid on the pan is essential, the water must not touch the bottom of the basin.
Method
Prepare the basin
Grease the pudding basin generously with soft butter. Pour {{golden-syrup}} of golden syrup into the base, tilting to coat evenly. Set aside.
Make the sponge batter
Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar. Use a hand mixer or wooden spoon. Beat for about four minutes, until pale and fluffy. This is the
creaming method
. The air you beat in here is what lifts the sponge when it steams.

Add {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. Beat in {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract. Sift in {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour and fold through with a large spoon until just combined. Add {{whole-milk}} of whole milk and fold again to give a batter that drops slowly from the spoon.
Fill and cover

Spoon the batter into the prepared basin over the golden syrup. Smooth the surface. Cover tightly with the basin lid or with a double layer of foil, pleated across the middle to allow the sponge room to rise. Secure with kitchen string tied under the lip.
Steam

Place a folded tea towel or a trivet in the base of a large saucepan. Set the basin on top. Pour boiling water into the pan until it reaches halfway up the sides of the basin. Bring to a steady simmer, cover the pan, and steam for 90 minutes. Check every 20 minutes and top up with boiling water as needed, the pan must not run dry.
Turn out and serve

Lift the basin from the water and rest for 5 minutes. Remove the cover and run a palette knife around the edge. Place a warmed serving plate over the top, invert firmly, and lift the basin away. The syrup pools around the base. Serve at once with crème anglaise or cold double cream.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Cream the butter and sugar for a full four minutes before adding the eggs. If the mixture curdles, add a tablespoon of flour before each remaining egg.
items.cause: Butter and sugar were not beaten enough, or the eggs were added too quickly, curdling the batter

items.fix: Ensure the water maintains a steady simmer throughout. If the sponge is underdone, replace the cover and steam for a further 20 minutes.
items.cause: Water in the pan fell below a simmer, or the basin cover was not sealed tightly enough
items.symptom: Sponge did not rise and is still wet in the centre

items.fix: Keep the water at a simmer, not a boil. A trivet or folded cloth under the basin prevents direct contact with the heat.
items.cause: The water touched the base of the basin, or the heat was too high, creating a rolling boil rather than a simmer
items.symptom: Golden syrup burns and turns bitter at the base

items.fix: Use softened (not melted) butter and press it into every curve of the basin before adding the syrup.
items.cause: The basin was not greased thoroughly enough

content.text: Variations

Replace the golden syrup with a 50/50 mix of golden syrup and black treacle for a darker, more intensely flavoured marmalade-pudding character. Add the finely grated zest of a lemon to the batter for a sharper note that cuts through the sweetness. Spotted dog is a variant of this same sponge with dried currants folded in, see the spotted dog recipe.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Steam the pudding up to two days ahead, cool completely in the basin, cover with cling film, and refrigerate. Reheat by steaming for 30 minutes. For the freezer, wrap the cooled basin tightly in cling film and freeze for up to three months; reheat from frozen by steaming for 45 minutes. Leftover steamed pudding reheats well in a microwave on medium power in 2-minute bursts.
Where this dish lives

Steamed puddings are the British contribution to cold-weather desserts. The Victorian household manual by Mrs Beeton gives them thirty pages, listing suet sponges, bread puddings, and rice puddings as the winter staples of any home kitchen. The golden-syrup version arrived with the wider sale of Lyle's product from the 1880s on. By the early twentieth century the treacle sponge had pushed many of the older jam-and-suet versions aside as the home standard. At its simplest, the dish shows what enclosed steam does to a batter that a dry oven cannot. The sponge stays moist all the way through and ends up pudding-soft inside.
